# Questions in Livestream 113

Live Stream #113: Questioning the Lore

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=449" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:29</a> | What should viewers make of the claim that the World Economic Forum called for an AI-written Bible? | He says there is no evidence the forum as a body called for that, even though AI tools can obviously generate text on demand. | He treats the story as fake news that sounded plausible to conspiracy-minded readers. His answer separates the general fact that anyone can prompt AI to produce a Bible-like text from the unsupported claim that the World Economic Forum collectively recommended doing so, and he adds that the forum has little record of enforcing its own recommendations anyway. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=747" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:27</a> | Why did a Twelfth Dynasty ruler use a name tied to Sobek, and was Hapy or even vegetables worshiped? | He ties the Sobek connection to the Fayum and the dynasty's move toward that region; Hapy was worshiped, but Egyptians did not generally worship vegetables. | He explains that Sobek was already an important Middle Kingdom deity and became especially prominent around the Fayum. Since the Dynasty 12 royal family had moved to Itjtawy, near a crocodile-rich region and major Sobek cult center, the royal use of Sobek names was understandable rather than anomalous. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=887" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:47</a> | What does Dr. Falk think of Finkelstein's and Sager's early Iron Age town population-density estimates? | He says archaeological demography is imprecise and densities vary; Egyptian settlements could be much denser than early Iron Age Levantine towns, so population estimates should be treated as ranges. | He does not object strongly to either estimate, but stresses that settlement-density calculations are estimates rather than precise counts. He contrasts early Iron Age Levantine towns with Egyptian examples such as Lahun, Deir el-Medina, and Avaris, where towns could be far more tightly packed, so any total population reconstruction should allow a range. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=1049" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:29</a> | How does the clan interpretation of Genesis 5 make sense of Enoch? | He treats Enoch within the clan structure and says the notice points toward a tradition of a group walking with God. | He reads Genesis 5 as clan-structured material and says Enoch can be understood as an original pious figure whose influence marks the clan associated with him. The shorter span of 365 years and the language of walking with God signal a faithful line within the antediluvian setting rather than merely an isolated oddity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=1347" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:27</a> | What is Dr. Falk's favorite prophecy and why? | He picks Isaiah 53 because he sees it as a suffering-servant passage that ties many Old Testament themes together in a messianic vision. | He walks through Isaiah 53 as a suffering-servant text, connecting it with Exodus imagery, Davidic-root language, Passover, vicarious atonement, guilt offering, and the righteous servant bearing iniquities. He likes it because it gathers many Old Testament themes into what he considers a powerful messianic vision. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=1690" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:10</a> | What are Dr. Falk's thoughts on Young's Literal Translation? | He calls it historically interesting and very literal, but too wooden for serious apologetics or exegetical work. | He describes Young's Literal Translation as an older translation designed to preserve grammar, syntax, and especially verbal tense. That makes it useful for seeing how a very literal approach works, but he says its wooden English can fail to communicate meaning well and is not a strong tool for apologetics or serious exegesis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=1959" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:39</a> | Does John 1:49 relate to Gnostic ideas of sparks becoming suns, or only to Stoicism's claim that Jesus was cosmic order itself? | He says the Logos language in John is older and broader than Gnosticism, drawing on Hellenistic Jewish and philosophical vocabulary rather than a simple Gnostic parallel. | He explains that the Greek term logos carried a long and broad history before later Gnostic systems, being used by Stoics, Pythagoreans, and other philosophical groups. In John, he treats the language as part of a wider Jewish and philosophical vocabulary about divine word, life, and light rather than evidence for a direct dependence on Gnostic spark mythology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=2233" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:13</a> | What is going on with the rods in Genesis 30:35-41; is it sorcery? | He argues Jacob is not doing magic but using bark with medicinal properties to strengthen the flock. | He frames the passage in the conflict between Jacob and Laban over the flocks, then argues the rods are not a magical breeding mechanism. His answer treats the peeled bark as a practical or medicinal intervention for the animals, with God still involved in the broader narrative outcome rather than Jacob performing sorcery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=2714" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:14</a> | How can Jacob fight God in Genesis 32:22-30 and somehow win? | He treats the scene as a divine encounter tied to Jacob's transformation and name change rather than a simple physical victory over God. | He notes that if God wanted a literal contest, Jacob could not overpower him; the story itself shows this when the figure disables Jacob by touching his hip. The point is Jacob clinging to the divine visitor for blessing, receiving a new name, and being marked by the encounter as he moves into a changed identity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=3117" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:57</a> | Is there a relationship between exorcising a demon by knowing its name and Egyptian ideas about knowing a god's true name? | He says yes; in the ancient Near East knowing a true name was associated with special power or access, though Yahweh cannot be manipulated that way. | He says ancient Near Eastern thought often treated a true name as granting access, power, or the ability to manipulate or drive out a being. He connects this to exorcism practice and to biblical warnings about misusing Yahweh's name, while emphasizing that Yahweh is not a deity who can be controlled. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=3387" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:27</a> | If Samuel's appearance to Saul was not a ghost, what exactly is a ghost? | He clarifies that Samuel could be called a ghost, spirit, or apparition, but "ghost" is a broader term than the narrower apparition language he prefers. | He says Samuel can be described as a returned spirit, ghost, or apparition, depending on how broadly the term is used. His caution is that "ghost" can cover many phenomena, including apparitions and psychic impressions, so he prefers the more specific language of apparition when discussing Samuel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=3549" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:09</a> | Does Dr. Falk have a favorite version of the Book of Common Prayer? | He does not use it much, but says he would probably choose the clearer 2019 version over the 1662 language. | He says he has used the Book of Common Prayer but does not have a strong personal attachment to one version. If choosing, he would likely prefer the 2019 version because English has changed so much since 1662 and the updated language is clearer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=3630" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:30</a> | What best explains the similarity between Ezekiel's four living beings and Mesopotamian apkallu or cherub imagery? | He says Ezekiel's imagery is much closer to Mesopotamian cherubim than apkallu and reflects later Mesopotamian influence on Israelite literature. | He distinguishes apkallu from cherub imagery, saying apkallu are often part-human, part-bird, or part-fish figures and only superficially overlap with the eagle element. Ezekiel's living beings, in his view, are much closer to Mesopotamian cherubim, which fits later Mesopotamian influence on Israelite literature. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=4076" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:56</a> | Why is Graham Hancock so popular when his ideas are not new? | He attributes the popularity to strong storytelling, saying fringe ideas gain traction when presented by engaging writers. | He says the ideas themselves are not new and compares Hancock with other fringe or revisionist writers whose arguments gain readers through narrative skill. Hancock's background as a journalist matters in his answer: good storytelling can persuade people even when the claims are factually weak. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=4259" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:59</a> | What does Dr. Falk make of Michael Heiser's view that accreditation for the Awakening School was unnecessary? | He agrees accreditation is unnecessary for certificates of completion, but says the school's marketing about "doctoral level" courses is misleading. | He agrees with Heiser insofar as the school offers certificates of completion rather than academic degrees, since such certificates do not require accreditation. His objection is to marketing language about "doctoral level" courses, which he considers misleading because it implies an academic status the certificates do not carry. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=4587" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:27</a> | Did Dr. Falk ever wish he lived in 1910-1920 so he could meet future kings and tribal leaders during fieldwork? | He says no, because those years include World War I; he would rather avoid that decade even if surrounding years were strong for archaeology. | He says he would not choose 1910-1920 because it would put him in the World War I years, including danger and disruption in the Middle East. He calls 1900-1910 and 1920-1930 better archaeological windows, but would give those advantages up to avoid the war decade. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=4726" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:18:46</a> | Is there literature explaining the beliefs of Continental young earth creationists? | He says they tend to produce tracts and small booklets through anti-Darwin societies rather than large American-style creationist books. | He says his experience with Continental young earth creationists came partly from living in England and encountering anti-Darwin societies. They are often less focused on publishing large systematic books or defending a strict young date and more focused on anti-Darwin material, usually in tracts, booklets, and small-press publications. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=4938" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:18</a> | Who are Dr. Falk's top three favorite church fathers, and why? | Off the top of his head he names Irenaeus, Athanasius, and Chrysostom, while noting the field is vast. | He prefaces the answer by saying the church fathers are a vast body of literature. His off-the-cuff favorites are Irenaeus, Athanasius, and Chrysostom, with Chrysostom singled out as especially interesting to read even though Falk jokes about not appreciating his insistence that parishioners stand. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=5029" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:49</a> | Has Dr. Falk read Mark S. Smith, and does he have anything to say about him? | He says Smith is an excellent scholar, though he disagrees with Smith's view of early Israelite polytheism and warns against quote-mining him. | He says he has read some Smith and regards him as a strong scholar, but warns that atheists and others often quote-mine him without representing the breadth of his argument. Falk disagrees with Smith's early-Israelite-polytheism model and leans more toward a Heiser-like divine council and monotheistic framework. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=5245" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:25</a> | Why does Dr. Falk refer to early pharaohs as kings, and is that common in Egyptology? | He says it is common because "pharaoh" as a title begins later, so earlier rulers are often called kings even though broad "pharaonic" language is also used. | He says Egyptologists commonly distinguish earlier "kings" from later rulers called "pharaohs" because "pharaoh" became a royal title around the reign of Thutmose III or Hatshepsut. Broad phrases like "pharaonic period" still get used, but for precise terminology Khufu is a king while later rulers can be both kings and pharaohs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=5574" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:54</a> | Has Dr. Falk seen the new Indiana Jones movie? | He says he has not seen it and is wary because later Indiana Jones films disappointed him. | He says he has not seen it and is afraid it will disappoint him, since he loved the first two films but felt the series declined afterward. He especially disliked Crystal Skull and does not want another Indiana Jones film to undermine his expectations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=5666" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:26</a> | What are Dr. Falk's thoughts on Dr. Michael Brown? | He says the lectures he has heard on slavery in the ancient Near East were quite good, though he does not know Brown's full background. | He says Brown seems fine based on the material he has heard, especially lectures on slavery in the ancient Near East, which he found quite good. He qualifies the answer by saying he does not know Brown's doctorate or broader scholarly background well enough to evaluate everything. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=5745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:45</a> | Can Dr. Falk elaborate on his eschatological position given his Old Testament background? | He downplays eschatology compared with orthodoxy and orthopraxy, then identifies himself as post-tribulational and historic premillennial. | He begins by saying eschatology is overemphasized and should come after orthodoxy and orthopraxy. When he does identify his own view, he describes himself as a post-tribulationist and historic premillennialist, while insisting that correct theology and right practice matter more than detailed end-times schemes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=6100" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:40</a> | What is the best approach when speaking to atheists? | He advises a friendly, genuine, sensitive approach that accounts for the person's background rather than rushing the exchange. | He says the approach depends on the person, including whether they were raised atheist or deconverted after hurtful experiences. His own practice is to be a real friend first, preserve the friendship, and discuss the Bible or Jesus when the person is open rather than treating the conversation as a rushed argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=6545" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:05</a> | Can Dr. Falk speak ancient Egyptian? | He says he can read and pronounce ancient Egyptian, but speaking a language is a separate skill and not the main scholarly need. | He distinguishes reading and pronouncing a language from speaking it conversationally, calling speaking an additional audio-vocal skill. He can read Egyptian and pronounce it as he reads, and can produce small examples, but says speaking it is not as important for his scholarly work as reading it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=6641" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:41</a> | What does Exodus 4:24 mean when the Lord sought to put Moses to death? | He says God was going to kill Moses because Moses had failed to circumcise his son before serving as covenant representative for Israel. | He says the passage means plainly that God was going to kill Moses. Moses was to lead Israel as the covenant representative of Abraham's people, yet he had not circumcised his own son, likely because of his Midianite family context, so the covenant sign became the crisis in the text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=6857" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:17</a> | Can we know how ancient Egyptians sounded? | He says we can know late Egyptian reasonably well through Coptic, but old and middle Egyptian pronunciation is less certain because of vowel and consonant shifts. | He says Coptic gives a strong basis for how late Egyptian sounded because it preserves Egyptian in a phonetic alphabet related to Greek. Middle Egyptian is less certain because the language changed and vowels have to be reconstructed, and he is cautious about reconstructions that impose a Semitic-style pattern too strongly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=7050" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:30</a> | What language are they speaking, or trying to speak, in Stargate SG-1? | He says it is a very butchered fictional development of Egyptian, not true Egyptian, though it appears to assume the language kept changing over time. | He says the show seems to imagine Egyptian continuing to develop and changing pronunciation over time, but the result is still badly distorted. He gives the example of a pronunciation correction in the show and says it is not true Egyptian, even if it uses some Egyptian-derived ideas. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=7181" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:41</a> | Were there any sounds Egyptians could not pronounce, and did they have an accent? | He says everyone has an accent, and Egyptians seem to have had difficulty distinguishing r and l sounds. | He says every language speaker has an accent, even if it is not strongly marked to them. The specific Egyptian phonological issue he identifies is difficulty with r and l sounds, which he compares to similar conflations in other languages. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=7539" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:39</a> | Was Moses' adoption by Egyptians an unusual practice? | He says no; adoption and fosterage arrangements were common, and both men and women could adopt in Egyptian society. | He says adoption was common in Egypt and could serve several purposes, including securing an heir or emancipating a slave through adoption and release. Adult men and women could adopt on their own authority, though he notes exceptions such as slaves and royal sons or daughters because of legal and palace-security concerns. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=7778" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:38</a> | What is Dr. Falk's favorite elevator argument for God's existence? | He says he does not really use arguments for God's existence and instead prefers a softer, pragmatic approach focused on the Bible's historicity. | He says he once studied philosophy of religion but found standard arguments for God's existence mainly convinced people already inclined to believe and provoked counterarguments from others. He now tends to start with the Bible's historical claims and let people decide, rather than relying on a short cosmological or teleological pitch. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=7949" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:29</a> | Was Ahaziah 22 the first time he ruled and 42 the second time, and how does that square with a one-year reign? | He checks the passages and says the issue appears textual; modern translations follow the reading that gives 22. | He checks 2 Kings 8:26 and 2 Chronicles 22:2 and notes that the text he is using has Ahaziah as 22 in both places. He suspects the 42 reading belongs to a textual-critical issue, but says he would need the exact second passage or textual evidence in front of him to analyze it fully. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=8433" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:33</a> | What should Christians know about the transmission of Scripture and how we get the modern Bible? | He emphasizes that English Bibles result from textual scholarship and translation choices, so translations are interpretations. | He says Christians should know that there is more than one textual variant and that English Bibles are the result of scholarly choices. Translation itself is interpretation, because a translator must understand one language and render it into another; even reading the original languages still involves interpretation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=8702" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:02</a> | Are the Septuagint numbers the most accurate? | He says Old Testament text criticism must be handled case by case; sometimes Septuagint numbers are better, sometimes not. | He rejects a blanket answer and says Old Testament textual criticism requires comfort with ambiguity. Sometimes the Septuagint preserves the better number, sometimes it does not, and the decision depends on the particular passage, manuscripts, and any confirming evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=9163" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:43</a> | What is the meaning of Acts 8:14-17, since it does not seem to support common Protestant or Catholic views of salvation? | He treats the passage as a special circumstance tied to Samaria, prophecy fulfillment, and showing that the Holy Spirit cannot be manipulated. | He acknowledges that the passage does not map neatly onto common Protestant or Catholic formulas, but treats it as a transitional special circumstance in Acts. The Samaritan setting, apostolic laying on of hands, and Simon's attempt to buy authority all show that the Spirit's gift is not a technique humans can control. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=9836" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:43:56</a> | When Egyptians put Anubis to bed, was he dog tired, and would the same question about Seth be more controversial? | He answers through the Anubis shrine cloak from Tutankhamun's tomb and says too little survives from Seth's known temple to know its practice. | He says Anubis was not literally put to bed, but an Anubis shrine could be covered with a cloak, as in Tutankhamun's tomb. On Seth, he says there is only one known Seth temple in this context, at Avaris, and too little survived to know whether comparable practices existed there. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=10213" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:50:13</a> | If Dr. Falk could make his own bumper sticker in any language, what would it say? | He would write it in hieroglyphs: "If you can read this, you're overeducated." | He chooses hieroglyphs because he thinks they are visually appealing and jokes that the text would read, "If you can read this, you're overeducated." He says he has wanted to make that into a bumper sticker, shirt, or similar design. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=10299" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:39</a> | When Yeshua died, did he ascend to heaven with the thief or descend to Sheol to preach to the souls? | He says the timing is complex, but Paradise language can include the blessed dead while Christ's own death precedes the thieves' deaths. | He says most interpreters associate Paradise with heaven, but Sheol was not necessarily a horrible place for the blessed dead. He emphasizes the timing complication: Jesus died before the thieves, so either the thief followed Christ to Sheol or Christ entered heaven first and the thief followed afterward. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=10544" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:44</a> | Why are only Job's daughters named? | He says the names likely point to Job's restored abundance and blessing, but notes he would need more study for the first name's significance. | He begins cautiously, saying he would need more time with the text to give a full answer. He explains that Hebrew names often carry literary significance, then connects Job's daughters' names to beauty, abundance, and Job's restored blessing, while leaving the first name less certain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=10960" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:40</a> | Are there good Eastern European Egyptologists or Bible scholars writing on early Exodus questions? | He knows good Eastern European Egyptologists, especially from the Czech Republic, but not good Bible scholars writing in that specific early Exodus area. | He says Eastern Europe has good Egyptologists, especially in the Czech Republic, and notes Czech work connected with sites relevant to early Exodus discussions. But he does not know Eastern European Bible scholars writing specifically in the early Exodus area and distinguishes that from knowing strong Egyptologists in the region. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=11371" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:31</a> | Do all Christians find the date of the Exodus touchy, or does that depend on church tradition? | He says it falls along denominational lines; mainstream Protestants, Catholics, and Orthodox often do not care, while evangelical and fundamentalist circles tend to be touchier. | He says the controversy is largely denominational and historical. Mainstream Protestant, Catholic, and Orthodox Christians often see little issue, while evangelical and fundamentalist circles are more attached to an early date, partly because the modern early-date dogma was popularized in North American anti-liberal scholarship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=11697" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:57</a> | What part did the Bronze Age collapse have in the decline and fall of the New Kingdom? | He says it affected Egypt through drought and regional disruption, reaching Egypt later and contributing to the end of Dynasty 20 and related economic stress. | He says the Bronze Age collapse affected the whole Fertile Crescent, but Egypt felt it differently and somewhat later than places like the Hittite Empire. Drought, regional disruption, and inflation begin to appear under Ramesses III and hit Egypt especially toward the end of Dynasty 20. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=12003" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:03</a> | Did Egyptians stop hyperinflation by increasing supply? | He says that in a barter economy, increasing the supply of goods from satellite territories was how inflation could be reduced. | He says the practical lever in a barter economy was to increase the supply of goods, especially from satellite territories. More goods would relieve supply pressure and bring inflation down, which he compares to "printing" more goods rather than printing money. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=12048" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:48</a> | What extra-biblical evidence is there for Joseph's land policy in Genesis 47? | He points to Egyptian land and tax arrangements, including priestly exemptions, as meaningful parallels. | He connects Genesis 47's fifth-tax and priestly-land exemption with Egyptian evidence from the early Dynasty 18 context, including Kamose and Ahmose-era material about high taxation and land arrangements. He says priestly holdings worked differently because they had their own land grants and were not taxed in the same way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=12379" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:26:19</a> | Did Joseph's land and tax policy get upended later? | He says it was changed but not fully redressed; the Dynasty 18 regime broke up land grants and distributed them, while taxation continued through the new landholders. | He says the Dynasty 18 regime redistributed land grants, so the policy changed but was not simply undone. Taxation continued through ownership structures: elites or officials held grants, parceled land to workers or farmers, taxed them, and then the king taxed the landholders. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=12522" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:42</a> | What were Amenhotep III's religious reforms, and why were they important? | He says the reforms reduced the number of deities at important cult sites and helped move the king toward greater solarization. | He says Amenhotep III's reforms had two purposes: reducing the number of deities worshiped at important cult centers and intensifying solar theology around the king. He uses the Amunet and Mut issue around Luxor and Karnak as an example of overlapping divine roles that the reforms tried to consolidate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=12762" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:42</a> | Which English Bible translation is most accurate for study purposes? | He especially commends the NASB 1977 for scholarly study and speaks favorably of the CSB as a strong modern study option. | He says the NASB 1977 is the most accurate translation he has found for scholarly study if someone cannot read Hebrew or Greek, though its English is not elegant. For personal Bible study or apologetics, he praises the CSB for balancing grammar, syntax, current scholarship, and thought-level translation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=12859" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:19</a> | What are Dr. Falk's thoughts on the NET Bible? | He says he has heard good things but has not read or evaluated it himself, so he cannot recommend it. | He says he has heard positive comments about the NET Bible but has not read or evaluated it himself and does not own a copy. Because his answer is based only on secondhand impressions, he will not recommend it until he has examined it directly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KSYXqKQnJxA?t=13949" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:29</a> | Has Dr. Falk ever played Shin Megami Tensei? | He says no; he has not had much time for video games recently. | He answers that he has never played it and has not had much time for video games recently. The surrounding discussion turns to a game-marketing offer he declined because he generally does not do product placements and would only consider something closely tied to ancient Near Eastern or historical content. |
